sentences.text: Into trains.
And they were going to take us to-- that's what we heard.
They were taking us to [INAUDIBLE] Mountains to eliminate us over there.
They didn't want to leave no-- no [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things in Germany.
They killed us right in Germany, you see.
So they put us into trains.
Where we were going?
No.
No.
No.
We didn't know where we were going. no.
Finally, we-- we stay a few days in the trains.
And all of a sudden, the train stop.
We take a look.
All the German SS, they're all gone.
And we see an old man from the Wehrmacht open the gates and we ran out in the city like wild beasts.
Everybody running into the wild beasts, running to the houses, grabbing some potatoes, you know, and all that.
We thought we had immigrated, you know.
Didn't last long.
Two hours later, the Luftwaffe comes in.
The Luftwaffe is people from the aircrafts.
The ground Luftwaffe came in with machine guns and-- what do you call them?-- and Jeeps, you know, little-- start shooting at us.
Back into the trains, Quite a few people died.
Put us back into the trains.
We stay there.
Already we got mixed up, you know, some-- you went in any wagon you can get in, you know.
It wasn't special, you know, like the one you were before.
It was all hullabaloo.
We had women with us, girls, you know, and everything else.
So finally, then they ask a few people-- after we went all in the trains, they surrounded everyone of us back.
They ask a few people to go down and bury the dead people, take them, pick them up from the streets and bury them.
I wasn't there, so I didn't go.
Some-- some of them, they did.
And then they brought them back into the-- into the wagons.
We stayed there a whole night.
And next day, the train started going, and all of a sudden stop in another place.
And we-- we stop over there.
We do not know was coming or going, you know.
But the-- the-- what do you call it?
The train that we were on had an anti-- anti-aircraft machine gun on top.
And all of a sudden, a bunch of airplanes comes in.
They start shooting us from the plane.
Was the Americans.
The Americans are shooting at us.
They didn't know who we were.
So finally what happened, they-- they give us some soup.
There was a big place.
They were giving soup to the guys.
And a lot of people got killed because-- from the airplane, they shot the people.
They were shooting right there, so a couple of friends of mine died there.
In the meantime, we took our prison coats.
We spread them out over the train, you know.
Then the Americans saw it, that it's killing prisoners.
They left us alone.
So in the meantime, quite a few people died by the American intentionally, of-- not intentionally, of course.
They didn't know that that day--
So then we went back to the-- the-- already no more SS.
No more SS.
That was near Tutzing, near Tutzing.
So the train-- We got back into the train, and it stopped in Tutzing, which is-- Tutzing is between Dachau, Munich, somewhere.
Is Within-- within an hour drive, in the neighborhood.
And all of a sudden we hear that the train cannot go any farther.
The French army were coming through one place and the 7th-- 7th army, which was Eisenhower, coming in through the other place.
The train got stuck, couldn't move.
No here, not there.
That's when we were liberated, right in the trains.
So we got liberated in the trains.
We don't know yet.
And all of a sudden, we see-- that was April 29, which is in the book.
We see the American tanks coming through, you know.
And we saw the star.
That was the best day of our lives, which we saw the Americans coming in.
And I met a Greek soldier into the tank.
You know, was in the tank.
So, you know, so I talked to him Greek.
And [INAUDIBLE] give us some food and so on.
But I was still in good shape.
A lot of people were so weak, you know, from the agony and here or there that they were very, very-- we had a lot of dead bodies on the-- on the train, you know.
So the German-- the Eisenhower came in.
And he sees what he saw, that he couldn't believe his eyes, you know.
And he brought in the Germans from the city to show what the Germans did to us.
And on the other side, the French came in from another section.
But the French just told us, come on, boys.
Let's go.
Let's kill a few Germans.
They were coming with machine guns, going to German houses.
But there was nobody down there.
With the machine guns, grab whatever you can, because the Greek Jews spoke French, you know, most of them.
And you were, you know-- we started-- became buddies, you know, singing French songs and so on.
It was sort of a nice gathering.
So same day, in the evening, all of a sudden they organized Feldafing.
Feldafing was a Hitlerjugend camp.
It was a camp strictly for Hitlerjugend, was big barracks and a few villas, you know, there.
So they took us over there.
And they give everybody pajamas to wear, because we were all filthy and so on.
I don't know, but they brought in a lot of pajamas.
But me, I went into the-- I ran away.
I was-- as soon as we got into Tutzing, I-- I ran away from the train, because we were free already.
And I went into Feldafing.
And I go inside-- what do you call the big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] warehouse.
They had a lot of clothes there.
They had everything.
So I get dressed like a Hitlerjugend and I brought a-- took a big-- what do you call-- a big drum.
And I started hitting and went back to-- to the trains, show my friends.
What happened?
All of a sudden I brought in a whole group of Greeks back, even before everybody came into the camp, because we were a couple of thousand people.
So all the Greeks started dressing up, you know, taking instruments, dressing up.
And we got into Feldafing.
And that was the liberation that we started.
That's what started the liberation.
And the German-- the Americans helped us a lot.
[INAUDIBLE] brought our food.
They gave us clothes.
And was no more hunger, that's it.
The main thing was the hunger that there were-- people were dying.
Some of the people, they had too much food to eat you know and they die from eating, because their stomachs were so empty.
And while they were eating, they were dying or got into diarrhea.
And that one started the first week of liberation.
And we were happy-go-lucky.
Of course, the Greeks always-- the Greek Jews always like to sing all songs from the early '40s, you know, when we started getting together and it didn't take long.
We-- we were well organized.
And some of the-- I was one of the youngest.
But I said, what would I do here in Germany?
I might as well go to Israel, you know.
Well, that was in April.
In-- I think in June or July, the Jewish Brigade came to Feldafing.
And he says, the kids-- which I was young-- take a bunch of us to take us to Israel.
So we went-- they took us from Feldafing, took us to Landsberg, a camp, another camp, DP camp, Landsberg which was, I don't know, a couple hours' drive.
We stayed there for a while.
And then they organized some trucks.
And they took us onto trucks.
And they crossed us into Italy through Firenzia.
Anyway, the Jewish Brigade had all the posts.
The Jewish Brigade was under the British army.
That was the Jewish brigade.
They were soldiers under the British army, people from Israel.
And they had all the posts in Italy, you know.
So the-- they sneaked us through, because it was illegal.
They sneaked us through to Italy.
We stay there in the city for a couple of days.
And they moved us into Modena.
Modena is a very small city in Italy, which is the Academy of Modena.
It's a very, very popular, very known academy, Modena, Academy de Modena.
It's very, very popular in Italy.
So there was a big huge, huge building.
Of course, we didn't have baths there, but we were sleeping with mattresses on the ground.
And we were happy-go-lucky already.
Hey, we are free in Italy.
We go to the streets.
There's lots of food, you know, and all that.
And we were living the life of Riley, the life of Riley in Italy, because we were hungry-- whatever we see was, you know, was OK.
Hey?
With my friends, a couple of friends.
As a matter of fact, I have their pictures here.
So in the meantime, when I crossed from Germany to Italy, I stole from the Hitlerjugend place about 100 undershirts with the-- undershirt with a black thing here.
They used to wear-- the Hitlerjugends used to wear that.
I stole about 100 of those and I took them with me to Italy.
And I used to sell them, one a day.
I used to have a-- [LAUGHS] a stake.
Clothing was very, very desirable in Italy, so-- and I was living the life of Riley, going to dancing and going with girls, let's put it this way, whatever it was that was available there.
And you know, young boy.
And I had a ball.
Finally, well, that was the first stop in Italy.
But to go to Israel, we had to go-- they took us to another city, Santa Maria di Bagni.
That was the name of the city.
And waiting there to go-- to send us to Bari, the port of Italy, to put us into boats and take us to Italy-- to Israel.
But in the meantime, I got involved black marketing.
And that's what my hobby is, always business.
You asked me in the beginning of the-- that was my hobby.
I started going from Rome to Milano by trains.
I didn't have to pay because I spoke in Spanish-- my-- and Italian and Spanish is very close.
And I used to go on the trains, no money.
I used to-- the conductor used to come says, [ITALIAN].
[SPANISH] I show him the number.
That was it.
Because, you know, the ghetto, you know.
So I used to have a ball.
Back and forth, sleeping in the trains, sleeping in the train stations.
I used to have an army coat, a big German army coat with those overcoats that they have.
That I used to put on the floor, sleep like nothing.
Didn't bother me a thing.
And I was-- and I was making money.
I was buying sugar one place, salt in the other place, trading around.
And I was not afraid to catch me, because [SPANISH] means I'm prisoner of war, so you can't do nothing.
There were nothing.
And I used to-- one day I'm in a-- in a city there.
And I see a big line up, soldiers, British soldiers there.
They line up to have food, you know, to get a ration.
So I [INAUDIBLE].
I'm in with my friend.
I says, I don't know nothing.
I go in the line too.
I stand with them, with the soldiers, you know.
[LAUGHS]
And all of a sudden, big officer comes in.
He says to me, hey, you, in English, but I would understood.
What do you want?
[SPANISH] Food.
He says, OK.
He puts me-- takes me inside, puts me at a table, you know.
And he gives me to eat.
And that was going off and on and I made a few bucks, you know.
I saved some money.
I made myself a beautiful suit in Italy, in-- I went back to Modena, because I used to go back and forth there, doing some business.
Bought myself a brand new piece of cloth.
I went to a tailor.
And I says, I want to have-- what do you call-- a suit made.
He says, OK.
You buy the material.
I'll make it for you no charge.
And I went to the barber.
I says, I want my hair, curly hair, make me-- I have a picture when I was liberated.
So I told my story to the Italian-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call-- tailor.
He started crying, the poor guy.
You know, he made me a suit, a gorgeous suit, silk, brown with stripes on it.
It was the--
So when I went to-- back to Santa Maria di Bagni, oh, well, he says, they called your number-- your name to go to Israel.
You weren't there so-- ah, I says, I'll take the next boat, you know.
Didn't bother me.
So I'll take the next boat.
So two or three times happen like this.
In the meantime, I was-- I felt the feeling, you know, there that I-- I can do well here, you know.
So I saved some money doing business.
And I figure, well, I'll go back to Germany.
After I was in Italy 'til December, I said, I'm going back to Germany.
I have some money.
I'll buy some leather goods in Germany, bring it back to Italy.
I can make good money on them, you know.
That's my--
So to go to Italy-- to go to Germany, I had to go through the mountains, because you can't cross legally.
It's illegal.
So I had some money in my pocket.
I had a rucksack with, you know-- and I go through the mountains of Bolzano, which is south Italy, through the mountains.

And I figure, well, the only way we can go back to Innsbruck-- you've got to go to Austria, then to Germany-- is through the footsteps from the previous guys, because we heard somebody has gone there.
So if they can do it, I can do it too.
So the only way you can cross the border-- I mean, the mountains, is by the footsteps, you know.
Sure enough, we had a snowstorm.
All the footsteps was wiped out.
We turn around with snow up to here.
We wander, wander, wander around, figure out, where do we land?
Back in Italy, but we don't know yet.
I go and ask the guy, where are we?
It was-- what a foolish man.
[LAUGHS] So he says, you know, he'll put us in jail that night.
But the next morning, we ran away.
So I went back to what do you call-- to Santa Maria di Bagni.
And the boys found out.
They-- they thought they-- they killed two guys on the train somewhere.
And they thought they'd kill me and my friend.
And it was Hanukkah.
You know Hanukkah, our holiday?
It was Hanukkah night.
And I go back into Santa Maria di Bagni, which is in December, end of December.
And I don't see anybody.
And all of a sudden, everybody is having a party upstairs.
And all of a sudden, I hear Greek songs, you know, like we always were liberty.
We always sing songs.
So as soon as I walked up there, the boys started jumping.
They said, they thought they kill you.
Ah, who's gonna kill us?
So in other words, we had a good evening that time.
I says, we got to go back to-- I got to go back to Germany.
So the second-- second attempt I made.
So we got into Innsbruck, me and my friend David.
And I had the money rolled up, like dollars rolled up in the seam of my-- what do you call it-- of my undershirt.
I hide them over here, you know.
Cross the border, sure enough, the French caught us.
My-- my friend had the money in a safe, a regular safe, you know.
But he had them in his pocket, you know.
So the French took the rucksacks, so they saw some-- we had some grappa, which is whiskey, nice-- so they took it.
They put the-- they searched me.
I didn't have anything, because everything was hidden.
So my friend's safe, took them.
He put them on the table, said, how you going to use this, eh?
But he didn't see the money inside, [BACKGROUND NOISE] you know.
Is somebody here?
So they found out it was-- they didn't catch it, so-- we got-- we got it back.
And we got back into Feldafing, back into the Feldafing.
Ah, I got sick from the mountains, you know, the snow and so on.
And a woman from the UNRRA-- which we had there, they were looking after us-- saw me.
And he says, you-- when I told them the story what happened, you know, through the mountains-- and I was sick, really sick, weak.
Says I'm going to send you for holiday.
He sends me for two weeks in Garmisch-Partenkirchen, which is a-- a ski resort, just about 80, 90 kilometers from Feldafing, in the mountains, a gorgeous place, like a palace.
So in the meantime, I had bought a lot of stuff to take back with me to Italy.
And I left everything else with the other Greeks in my-- in the camp.
So when I got-- and I had a few bucks.
And I went to this holiday in Garmisch-Partenkirchen, beautiful white sheets and nurses to look after you and going with the snow-- with the slates early in the morning.
It was gorgeous.
And I started-- I mean, I was a gambler.
I always like to play money.
Money was to me-- and-- and all of a sudden I start playing poker over there.
So I got to like it, so I asked permission if I can stay two more weeks.
Instead of two weeks, I stay four.
And I made quite a bit of money playing cards with other people there.
So I come-- I come back to Feldafing after my holiday to figure, well, now it's time for me to go back to Italy.
I go back into my camp.
They stole my suitcase with all my stuff.
Now I have nothing now left, except the money that I made in the-- what do you call-- into the winter resort.
So I figure, well, I left my-- what do you call?-- my suit and all the stuff in Italy, you know, to-- on another friend.
So now what should I do?
Should I go back?
So I was saying, no, I'm not going back.
So one of the other fellows came in from Italy that was going back to-- he was Germany.
He was going back to Italy.
Say, you know something?
I have this and this and this and this and that.
You want to give me some money, you take it over there?
I'll sell it to you, you know.
So I sold him whatever I had in Italy.
He paid me in Germany.
And I started doing business, black market again.
So I started-- all of a sudden already we had-- we're talking about now quite a few months.
There were some babies around.
Some kids came in from different camps.
I started-- I opened myself a big-- took a big table in the camp.
Feldafing was only Jewish people, of course.
And I was-- I went to Germany, in Munich.
And I was buying from the Germans shoe polish, ladies' garters, many-- combs, you know, things that everybody needed, you know, because we didn't have any.
So I put a big table and I was selling in and out.
Then I was selling safes.
You know, like I used to blow shapes out of balloons, you know.
That's the kind of a business I was doing.
That started pretty good.
Then in the-- in camp, in Feldafing, I opened myself a little store, a mini store.
I made-- I built a store.
Like a six by six, made of wood, you know.
And that was the first store was built in Feldafing, which was against the law.
They were not allowed to.
But I had a Greek behind me.
They were all my friends.
They were all tough.
Greeks were very, very tough after liberation.
The Greeks who survived was the-- not the cream of-- it wasn't the cream of Salonica.
It was the rough Salonica.
These people were stevedores.
They were hard people.
Like lawyers and doctors and bookkeepers never survived.
They died right away in Birkenau or any other camps.
But the people who survived, the pictures you got over there, you know, all the-- they're all-- none of them is a lawyer or a doctor.
We're all working guys, poor people.
And they survive, you know.
So my store stayed intact.
They wouldn't touch me.
And from there on, I built another store, two stores.
I had two partners with me, which they're still alive.
The one, he just died six months ago in Florida.
The other one's still alive.
And I used to go to Stuttgart, different cities, and bring different types of goods and sell it to the people in camp.
And then I put up two or three other Greeks.
I put them in little stands.
I used to sell them.
They could sell it, you know, to make a buck.
And it was going beautifully.
I had lots of money.
I was one of the richest kid in-- in the camp, you know.
I had money.
I'll tell you the truth.
No.
As I told you before at the beginning, I sold everything before they took us.
Remember, I told you I sold the sewing machine, my mother's things.
My-- I didn't know my brother was alive, to start of with, to start off.
And secondly--
Perished.
And what would I go back to Greece for?
I have-- we didn't leave no properties.
We didn't have any properties, because we were-- we never own any property, like a house or some of this.
We never did own.
And whatever we had, we-- I sold everything during the '41 to '43.
So I figure, what will I go back to Greece for?
So I might as well go to Israel.
That was my intent, to go to Israel.
But when I was in Italy, I was ready to go to Greece with a friend of mine is in the picture here, Harry Levy.
He was a well-off in Greece.
He was a rich boy.
And he says to me, El, come with me back to Greece.
Whatever I find, I'll give you half, which at the time, he could say anything.
But you know something, you know, he talked me into it.
And I was ready in Bari.
You know, I went to Bari to get the boat to go to Athens.
And all of a sudden, [INAUDIBLE], I says, you know, sometimes I'm very-- I can tell something is wrong, you know what I mean?
And I says to him, Harry, you know something?
You go.
See how things are.
If things are OK, you let me know.
I can take next boat.
So I took my suitcase.
I went back to Santa Maria die Bagni.
He went back to Greece.
And he found my brother alive.
So my brother sends me a telegram and says El, don't come to us.
Don't come to Greece.
It's rough here.
It's very, very hard.
Try to go to Israel or the States.
That was the end of Greece for me, you see.
So I-- I didn't-- I had no intentions to go back to Greece whatsoever.
But in Feldafing, I was-- l was like a king.
'Til June, 1948.
I stayed all the time in Feldafing.
Well, then I went to-- what do you call-- to come to Canada.
I went to [INAUDIBLE], a quarantine hotel to go through-- through what do you call-- through papers.
And then I made the-- the funny thing was that in 1946, I wanted to go to the States.
Canada didn't exist at the time.
There was no transfer for Canada.
And they were sending a lot of kids under 18 to United States, because, you know, because Canada, I really didn't know too much about Canada.
But America, there's always America, you know, trees on the street.
You could pick the money.
So-- and I was ready.
You know, I arranged for myself to go to the-- to United States.
Everything was fine.
You know, I was-- the age was OK.
I was-- at the time I was 18 years old, you know.
And it's time.
So what was-- we are ready to go to the consulate, to the American consulate to get our visa to go to the United States.
They found out there is no-- Greek quota doesn't exist.
You're supposed to-- you must have a quota to go to the United States.
And Greek quota, you have to wait five years before a quota will be, available even though I was displaced person.
So that said, that became, United States, that was Elia [GREEK] that you saw in the picture there.
I was with him.
The two of us were the first two Greek Jews living Germany to go to the United States.
He was broke, and I [INAUDIBLE] him, you know.
I helped him a lot.
But we never made it, so we came back to Feldafing.
Then after-- that's in 1946.
And then I went into business in Feldafing.
I was doing well.
I didn't care so much about-- then we heard Canada, the Jewish Congress, Canadian Jewish Congress is taking kids under 18.
That's when in comes out that I was 20.
I couldn't make it.
So that's what I got.
And I changed my name.
I changed everything else.
And--
Well, there was an Ukrainian girl working for the Canadian embassy in Munich, so I-- I bought her a fur coat.
And she came with a typewriter into Feldafing and she made my papers.
And that's it.
Yeah.
OK.
